coredata.dc:title: Supercritical water in top-down formation of tunable-sized graphene quantum dots applicable in effective photothermal treatments of tissues
coredata.dc:creator: Tayyebi, Ahmad
Akhavan, Omid
Lee, Byeong-Kyu
Outokesh, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: Supercritical water was used for simultaneous fragmentation and reduction of graphene oxide (GO) sheets into water-dispersible graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with tunable sizes. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) demonstrated that by increasing the temperature above the critical point of water, the average size and thickness of the GQDs were decreased and the size uniformity and production yield were increased. The results of thermal conductivity measurement of GQD nanofluids with different weight fractions indicated that the GQDs prepared at supercritical condition could enhance the thermal conductivity of water by 65% as compared to 35% for the GQDs synthesized at sub-critical conditions. Near-infrared (NIR) photothermal induction of a tissue-equivalent model (agarose gel) treated by the graphene nanomaterials showed that using the GQDs significantly increased the thermal conductivity (∼9.6 times), absorption coefficient (25%) and temperature uniformity (∼5.4-fold lower temperature difference) of the tissue in comparison with GO sheets. These results support the promising application of these GQDs in more efficient photothermal therapeutic purposes requiring lower thermal stresses.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A and B) sub-GQDs, (C and D) near-GQDs and (E and F) sup-GQDs water conditions. The Insets show size distribution of GQDs.
AFM topography of (A) sub-GQDs and (B) Sup-GQDs. Inset showing height profile of white marked-line.
(A) Raman spectra, (B) FTIR spectra, (C) O/C ratio and (D) XPS deconvolution of C1s core level for synthesized materials.

UV-visible absorbance of (A) GQDs aqueous solutions (B) PL spectra of GQDs samples, and PL emission spectra (recorded for progressively longer excitation wavelengths from 320 to 460 nm) of in water solution for (C) near-GQDs, and (D) sup-GQDs (Optical photograph).

(A) Thermal conductivity enhancement for GO and prepared GQDs at different temperature, (B) The phantom temperature profiles versus time, and the inside (T1) and outside (T2) temperature of tissue models exposed to NIR laser irradiation at various irradiation times for the tissues containing (C) GO and (D) sup- GQDs.

head.ce:title: Supercritical water in top-down formation of tunable-sized graphene quantum dots applicable in effective photothermal treatments of tissues

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil and Environment Engineering, University of Ulsan, Daehakro 93, Namgu, Ulsan, 680-749, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Civil and Environment Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Sharif University of Technology, P.O. Box 11155-9161, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Energy Engineering, Sharif University of Technology, P.O. Box 11365-8639, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supercritical water was used for simultaneous fragmentation and reduction of graphene oxide (GO) sheets into water-dispersible graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with tunable sizes. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) demonstrated that by increasing the temperature above the critical point of water, the average size and thickness of the GQDs were decreased and the size uniformity and production yield were increased. The results of thermal conductivity measurement of GQD nanofluids with different weight fractions indicated that the GQDs prepared at supercritical condition could enhance the thermal conductivity of water by 65% as compared to 35% for the GQDs synthesized at sub-critical conditions. Near-infrared (NIR) photothermal induction of a tissue-equivalent model (agarose gel) treated by the graphene nanomaterials showed that using the GQDs significantly increased the thermal conductivity (∼9.6 times), absorption coefficient (25%) and temperature uniformity (∼5.4-fold lower temperature difference) of the tissue in comparison with GO sheets. These results support the promising application of these GQDs in more efficient photothermal therapeutic purposes requiring lower thermal stresses.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) or nanometer-sized graphene structures have attracted re-search interest because of their unique combination of several key merits, including small size, essentially carbon-based biocompatibility [1] (although some potential cytotoxicities were also observed) [2], high thermal conductivity [3], excellent near-infrared (NIR) absorption [4], and water-solubility [5]. Moreover, such nanometer-sized graphene structures promise various applications in bioimaging [6], photothermal or photodynamics therapy [7], biosensing [8] and nanofluidics [3]. GQDs have been synthesized by fragmentation/cutting of graphene sheets and organizing of well-defined molecular structures, categorized as “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches, respectively [9,10]. Among several techniques developed for GQD synthesis using “top-down” approach, the two-step acid oxidation and cutting of graphene sheets has become more popular, due to the simplicity and inexpensiveness. Until now, hydrothermal [11], solvothermal [12], and microwave [13] cutting methods have been studied for GQD fabrication. The fragmentation of graphene into nanoscale sheets through sonication of graphene sheets locally functionalized by massive molecules was also reported [14]. Nevertheless, high-yield and environmentally friendly cutting methods are still required for GQD production, especially with tunable sizes.

Here, we report high-yield GQD fabrication with uniform and tunable sizes, e.g., 2–6 nm, using super-critical water. Subcritical (T = 250 °C), near critical (T = 370 °C) and supercritical (T = 400 °C) water were also used to further study the effect of temperature and water phase on the size, thickness, and thermal conductivity of the GQDs. For simplicity, these samples are denoted as sub-GDQs, near-GQDs and sup-GQDs, respectively. Due to the unique properties of supercritical water, including low dielectric constant, high specific heat capacity, and high pressure [15], the molecules of supercritical water take part in the breakage of chemical bonds (either carbon-oxygen bonds resulting in reduction of graphene oxide (GO) or carbon-carbon bonds resulting in fragmentation of GO) by significantly lowering the activation energy. The thermal conductivity of the synthesized GQDs was measured in a nanofluidic system. Then, the capability of the GQDs inserted in agarose gel for NIR laser absorption and heat production, dissipation and transfer was evaluated.

The Synthesis procedure of GQDs, and tissue-equivalent model were discussed in supplementary information. The starting material was well-known GO sheets (with typically microscale lateral dimensions) which were transformed into reduced GO (rGO) sheets by using a supercritical water reduction method. Figure S2a and S2b show transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of GO and rGO sheets, respectively. Figure S2c also shows successful cleavage of graphite into monolayer GO sheets with microscale lateral dimensions, by using Hummers' method.

Fig. 1 shows TEM images of the GQDs synthesized from GO by using various conditions of critical water at different temperatures of 250, 370, and 400 °C, corresponding to subcritical, near-critical and supercritical conditions, respectively. The TEM images indicate that by increasing the temperature or equivalently by changing the water phase from subcritical to supercritical, the GQDs became more monodispersed with lower average sizes. In fact, the average size of the sub-GQDs, near-GQDs and sup-GQDs was 55 ± 5, 5 ± 0.4, and 3 ± 0.2 nm, respectively. The sub-GQDs not only showed larger average diameter, but their monodispersity significantly decreased (Fig. 1A, B and Figure S4). This demonstrated that temperature can act as a simple factor for controlling the lateral size and monodispersity of the GQDs. The higher density of the GQDs at supercritical condition (Fig. 1e) also showed higher yield production in comparison with the near-critical and subcritical conditions. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis represent a good agreement with TEM images in which the hydrodynamic sizes of the sup-GQDs is 2–5 nm in diameter (Figure S3). Furthermore, the size measurement of the near-GQDs (∼3–6 nm) and the sub-GQDs (∼30–100 nm) displayed that the particle size simultaneously increased at lower temperature (Figure S3A –S3C). The zeta potential of the GQDs at different temperatures was also investigated. As shown in Figure S3, the zeta potential changed from −6 mV to −3 and −2 mV for the sub-GQDs, near-GQDs and sup-GQDs, respectively.

To further investigate the change in both height and lateral dimensions of the GQDs, atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis was utilized. Fig. 2 A and B shows AFM images and their height profile analysis. The AFM image of the sub-GQDs (Fig. 2A) shows the low density of the GQDs with thicknesses corresponding to ∼3–5 monolayers. At supercritical condition, a higher density (indicating higher yield production) and thicknesses of ∼1 nm corresponding to 1–2 monolayers [16] are observable (Fig. 2B).

Based on the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns (Figure S5), microscale GO sheets showed a crystalline structure, while the GQDs presented a collective amorphous property. In fact, the single GQDs obtained at supercritical condition showed two d-spacing of 0.12 and 0.21 nm, corresponding to (002) and (001) lattice planes, respectively, in good agreement with the SAED pattern. The higher graphitization of the sup-GQDs compared to the sub-GQDs is also shown in their Raman spectrum (Fig. 3 A). As seen, the ordered G band at 1582 cm−1 was stronger than the disordered D band at 1372 cm−1 with smaller D to G intensity ratio of 0.9 for the sup-GQDs compared to 1.07 for the prepared sub-GQDs. The FTIR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the samples further confirmed the deoxygenation of the sheets during supercritical water treatment. As shown in Fig. 3B, the hydroxyl group at 1380 cm−1 became very weak and the carboxyl group (COOH) at 1720 cm−1 and the epoxy groups at 1040 cm−1 disappeared for the sup-GQD sample. The survey XPS showed that with increasing temperature the amount of oxygen functionalities decreased (Figure S6), with the O/C ratio decreasing from 67% for GO to 17% for the sup-GQD (Fig. 3C). Moreover, deconvolution of the C1s peak of the samples indicated that the fitted peak assigned to C=O groups at 288 eV disappeared and the signal assigned to C-O groups became weak after supercritical water treatment.

The UV-vis absorption of the prepared samples provides valuable information regarding their structural properties. For the GO sheets, the absorption peak at 230 nm, relating to π-π transition of aromatic sp2 domains, was detected (Figure S7). For the GQDs samples, this peak shifted to higher wavenumbers (∼260 nm), corresponding to the deoxygenation of the GO at supercritical conditions. In addition to this shift, a new absorption peak appeared at 360 nm (Fig. 4 A) after changing the water phase from subcritical to near-critical condition. At the supercritical condition, the intensity of this new peak significantly increased (Fig. 4A), corresponding to the higher yield formation and mono-dispersibility of the GQDs. Inset Fig. 4A shows the stability of the synthesized GQDs by comparison the UV-Vis absorbance of sup-GQDs solution after 1 and 2 months. As it can be seen in inset Fig. 4A, the UV-vis absorbance shows very little change in comparison with time in long time. Furthermore, the luminescence properties of green emission sup-GQDs measured by time in which the fluorescence emission almost same after 2 months of sample preparation. These results clearly demonstrated the stability of synthesized GQDs in the aqueous solution.

The PL emission of the near-GQDs (excitation wavelength = 320 nm) appeared at 450 nm, giving blue luminescence (inset of Fig. 4C). The PL spectrum of the sup-GQDs (Fig. 4B) was extended from 450 to 550 nm. The sub-GQDs showed only a very weak PL maximum at 440 nm. The PL quantum yield of sub-GQDs, near-GQDs and sup-GQDs are measured using quinine sulfate as a reference. Figure S8 shows the quantum yield of sup-GQDs enhanced up to 7.6% as compared with 0.1 and 5.4% of sub-GQDs and near-GQDs, respectively. This enhancement of quantum yield possibly attributed to the higher order of reduction of GQDs at near and supercritical conditions as it is shown in Fig. 3C. Interestingly, the sup-GQDs and the near-GQDs showed two different excitation-dependent PL emissions (Figs. 4C and 5D). When the excitation wavelength was changed from 320 to 460 nm, the PL peaks of the near-GQDs shifted from 450 to 510 nm, whereas the emission peak of the sup-GQDs was nearly unaffected by the excitation wavelength. In addition, the sup-GQDs demonstrated high fluorescence properties in the high excitation wavelength, while the near-GQDs fluorescence properties almost disappeared at higher excitation wavelength (>400 nm).

The thermal conductivity and NIR laser photothermal property of the GQDs were studied, as compared to the GO sheets. The effect of different weight percentages of the GQDs on the thermal conductivity of water was investigated. As depicted in Fig. 5A, the thermal conductivity of nanofluids containing the GO enhanced 17% as compared with DI water. After cutting-down micro-sized GO sheets into nano-sized reduced graphene sheets, sub-GQDs (Average lateral size = 55 nm) become two-fold more efficient in heat dissipation relative to GO nanofluids. The thermal conductivity of GQDs nanofluids was improved up to 60 and 65% for near-GQDs and sup-GQDs, respectively. This possibly attributed to smaller size of GQDs (especially those prepared at the supercritical condition) and deoxygenation effect of supercritical water [17]. Therefore, sup-GQDs show promising materials for dissipation of generated heat after photothermal therapy and avoid heat damage during photothermal therapy process.

To investigate the photothermal property of the GQDs in a tissue-equivalent medium, they were injected into agarose gel phantoms at 10 mg GQDs or GO per 1.0 g of agarose gel (Figure S9A). Then, the injected samples were exposed to irradiation of 1600 mW at a wavelength of 800–810 nm and focal spot diameter of 2 mm. Two separate thermometers were used for detecting both inside and outside temperatures of the phantoms, as shown in Figure S9B. Two factors which possibly contributed directly or indirectly in generation of heat in GQDs samples are discussed in supplementary information (Figure S10). High optical absorption in NIR region (800–810 nm) and inelastic scattering of incident laser are two factors resulted in heat generation in NIR region. As it Fig. 5B displays the average temperature of different phantoms (Tave.) as a function of time (See A1 in Appendix for calculating average temperature). It shows that the phantoms containing the sup-GQDs attained a temperature of 45 °C after 460 s irradiation, in comparison with 40 °C for the GO sample.

As shown in Fig. 5B, it can be inferred that the amount of generated heat in tissue equivalent medium by NIR laser irradiation are equivalent for sup-GQDs and GO (Qave. = m × C × ΔTave.). Fig. 5C presents the differences between the inside and outside temperatures of the graphene-containing tissue medium exposed to NIR irradiation. The temperature difference for GO was very high (∼16.2 °C), compared to only ∼3 °C for the sup-GQDs (Fig. 5D). This was attributed to the higher thermal conductivity of the sup-GQDs in comparison with the GO sheets. In addition, the nanoscale size and capability of the homogenous dispersion of the sup-GQDs in the tissue model provided better heat transfer resulting in quick and efficient heat dissipation and consequently lower temperature gradient avoiding formation of local hot spots in tissue during photothermal therapy process (nearly uniform distribution of temperature across the tissue).

In order to further study the thermal properties of the GQDs in the tissue model, a mathematical method was developed (See A2. in Appendix) and solved by “COMSOL Multiphysics” software (Figure S11). The simulation results showed an appreciable agreement with the experimental data (Figure S12) in which thermal conductivity of the tissue model after injection of the sup-GQDs enhanced 10-fold in comparison with GO (Table S1). Furthermore, light absorption coefficient of sup-GQDs increased up to 70 m−1 in comparison with 56 m−1 for GO tissue model.

In summary, supercritical water was applied, for the first time, in a top-down fabrication of tunable-sized GQDs, through simultaneous fragmentation, deoxygenation and further exfoliation of GO sheets. The synthesized GQDs induced a significant increase in the optical absorption coefficient, thermal conductivity, and temperature uniformity of the tissue models locally exposed to NIR laser irradiation, as compared to known GO sheets. These unique properties of the synthesized GQDs can prevent the formation of high gradient temperatures across the tissues (which often occur in common laser photothermal therapy of tumors, inducing strong thermal stresses and serious damages of tissues). Therefore, these GQDs are a promising material for nanotechnology-based photothermal therapy with low thermal side effects.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: OA thanks the Research Council of Sharif University of Technology for partial financial support of the project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

Experimental details, simulation equations, AFM image of the GQDs, HRTEM and SAED of the GQDs, and “COMSOL Multiphysics” simulation results.
Supplementary data Supplementary data

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.12.057.
